coredata.dc:title: One-step microwave synthesis of N,S co-doped carbon dots from 1,6-hexanediamine dihydrochloride for cell imaging and ion detection
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Caihe
Deng, Zhiqin
Chen, Jiucun
Jin, Yanzi
coredata.dc:description: As a new member of the fluorescent carbon nanomaterial family, carbon dots (CDs) not only have outstanding photoluminescence properties and small size characteristics, but also contain favourable low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility, which could be the best choice to detect of ions to replace quantum dots for ions detection. Here, the N,S co-doped carbon dots (N/S-CDs) was synthesized by one-step microwave using 1,6-hexanediamine dihydrochloride and dimethyl sulfoxide as precursors, and their morphology and structure were characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS and FTIR. The optimal emission wavelength of the CDs was 512 nm with green fluorescence, and was red-shifted gradually as the excitation wavelength aggrandized. The synthesized CDs owned a well quantum yield of 24 %. It was further applied for the detection of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− with an excellent detection limit of 0.34 μM and 0.23μM, respectively. Cr2O7 2− did not influence the N/S-CDs PL response of MnO4 − in the presence of excessive Pb2+. Moreover, the obtained N/S-CDs demonstrated preeminent biocompatibility and could be resoundingly applied for cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave synthesis
Carbon dots
Cell imaging
Ion detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities ( SWU118035 and XDJK2019B001 ), the Transformative Project for Excellent Scientific and Technological Achievements in University ( KJZH17108 ) and the Special Program for Chongqing Social Business and People’s Livelihood Guarantee of Science and Technology ( cstc2017shmsA30001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the synthesis process of the N/S-CDs.
TEM (a) and XRD (b) spectra of the N/S-CDs. The XPS spectra of N,S-CDs (c) and C1 s (d), N1 s (e) and O1 s (f) S2p (f).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) Emission spectra of the N/S-CDs.

(a) The selectivity of negative ions, (b) Fluorescence spectra of different concentration MnO4 −, (c) a linear relationship as the concentration of MnO4 − ranges from 1 to 20 μM and (d) Fluorescence spectrum of MnO4 − after precipitation Cr2O7 2− of the N/S-CDs.

(a–m) Fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells labeled with the N/S-CDs magnification of the microscope. (a, d, g and j) bright field; (b, e, h and k) excitation at 410, 460, 480 and 530 nm, respectively; (c, f, i and m) the superposition of the bright field and excitation. (Each scale bar is 50 μm and the concentrations of these CDs are 200 μg mL−1.).

head.ce:title: One-step microwave synthesis of N,S co-doped carbon dots from 1,6-hexanediamine dihydrochloride for cell imaging and ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Technologies of Clean Energies, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Technologies of Clean Energies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Technologies of Clean Energies, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Engineering Research Center for Micro-Nano Biomedical Materials and Devices, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Engineering Research Center for Micro-Nano Biomedical Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Engineering Research Center for Micro-Nano Biomedical Materials and Devices, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new member of the fluorescent carbon nanomaterial family, carbon dots (CDs) not only have outstanding photoluminescence properties and small size characteristics, but also contain favourable low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility, which could be the best choice to detect of ions to replace quantum dots for ions detection. Here, the N,S co-doped carbon dots (N/S-CDs) was synthesized by one-step microwave using 1,6-hexanediamine dihydrochloride and dimethyl sulfoxide as precursors, and their morphology and structure were characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS and FTIR. The optimal emission wavelength of the CDs was 512 nm with green fluorescence, and was red-shifted gradually as the excitation wavelength aggrandized. The synthesized CDs owned a well quantum yield of 24 %. It was further applied for the detection of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− with an excellent detection limit of 0.34 μM and 0.23μM, respectively. Cr2O7 2− did not influence the N/S-CDs PL response of MnO4 − in the presence of excessive Pb2+. Moreover, the obtained N/S-CDs demonstrated preeminent biocompatibility and could be resoundingly applied for cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Trace anions play an essential role in various fields such as biology, chemistry and environment. The detection of these anions has become one of the important research fields [1–3]. However, the application of various detection methods has encountered challenges due to the diversity and complexity of ions. Among various detection technologies, fluorescence detection method is easy operation with good selectivity, sensitivity and response time, and thus this method is widely used in the field of biological detection [3–7]. Although traditional fluorescent chemical sensors can also detect biomolecules, most of these probes are based on small organic molecules. Some small molecular probes are complex in design with poor water solubility, and cannot detect molecules in pure aqueous phase [8]. As a new form of fluorescent luminescent material, fluorescent nano-materials have obvious advantages compared with traditional organic dyes, such as good water solubility and light stability, so they are widely used in ion detection fluorescent sensors [9–12]. The fluorescent sensor of the traditional quantum dot has a potential impact to the environment and human health because of the presence of heavy metal atoms with strong toxic and side effects, so it is difficult to be clinically applied [13–15].

As a novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) possess outstanding photoluminescence properties and small size characteristics with favourable low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility, which are the best choice for replacing quantum dots [16–19]. For instance, Feng et al. synthesized carbon dots through hydrothermal method employing ethylenediamine and citric acid [20]. The fluorescence response of CDs for NO2 − was studied at different pH, manifesting the CDs synthesized by this method can be used in the quantitative analysis of NO2 −. The blue fluorescence N, B co-doped carbon quantum dots were acquired in our previous study using polyethylenimine and 4-formylphenylboronic acid [21]. Then it was used for the detection of Fe3+ in solution, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.62 μM. Furthermore, we successfully synthesized N, S co-doped CDs by hydrothermal method employing cystamine dihydrochloride and citric acid as precursor, and the quantum yield (QY) of CDs reached up to 39.7 % [22]. The N, S/C-dots was applied to the detection of Cr2O7 2−, and the LOD was 0.86 μM.

Here, we successfully synthesized the N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N/S-CDs) using 1,6-hexanediamine dihydrochloride and dimethyl sulfoxide. The optimal wavelength of emission for the N/S-CDs was 512 nm with a quantum yield of 24 %. Furthermore, we used it for the detection of MnO4 − with a LOD of 0.34 μM. Finally, cell imaging of the N/S-CDs was also successfully photographed, demonstrating its excellent biocompatibility.

1,6-hexanediamine dihydrochloride was bought from Sigma-Aldrich, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was bought from Richjoint Chemical Reagents. 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl) -2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were obtained from Gibco, and human breast cancer cell (MCF-7) was purchased from the cell repository of Chinese Academy of Sciences. Total reagents were obtained from Shanghai, China. The purity of all reagents was analytically pure and has not any purification. Ultrapure water was rooted in the Milli-Q.

The morphologies and size of the CDs were measured with transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100). The degree of crystallinity of the N/S-CDs was measured with X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu, XRD-700). The fluorescent property of CDs was researched by UV–vis spectra (Shimadzu, UV-2550) and a spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, RF-5301PC). The surface construction of the CDs was obtained through fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Scientific, Nicolet 6700) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific, Escalab 250xi). The thermal stability of the preparation CDs was characterized by thermogravimetry analysis (TGA, Q50) under a N2 atmosphere with the warming rate of 10 °C min−1. Cell imaging was characterized by laser confocal fluorescence microscopy (LCFM, Nikon ECLIPSE Ti-s).

0.378 g of 1,6-hexanediamine dihydrochloride was dispersed in 35 mL of DMSO. After completely dissolving of 1,6-hexanediamine dihydrochloride, this solution was heated using a microwave at 180 °C for 35 min. After cooling to room temperature, this solution was filtered employing filter membrane (0.22 μm) and dialyzed (500 Da) in deionized water for 36 h. After further using freeze-drying, the N/S-CDs powder was gained.

The QY of N/S-CDs was obtained in line with a classic slope method by using Rhodamine 6 G (QY = 95 %) as the contrast substance, on the basis of following equation [23]:
Qx = Qr (Ix /Ir ) (nx /nr )2
where Q is the quantum yield, I is the slope depended on the photoluminescence intensity and absorption value, and n is the refractive index of solvent. The subscript “r” and “x” refers respectively to reference substance and synthetic sample. In this experiment, all solvents were anhydrous ethanol, so nx /nr = 1.

Various concentrations of MnO4 − (0–600 μM) were respectively prepared by dissolving KMnO4 in DI water. The MnO4 − solutions (1 mL) were separately added into the N/S-CDs solutions (1 mL, 1 mg mL−1). Then the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at 460 nm after mixing for 5 min. In addition, different anions (Cl−, ClO4 −, ClO−, NO2 −, Cr2O7 2−, Br−, P2O7 4−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−) were studied.

The MCF-7 cells were aliquot in 96-well plates and incubated for 12 h in an incubator. The cell culture fluid with different N/S-CDs concentrations (0-400 μg mL−1) was injected into the 96-well plate and hatched for 24 h. After removal of the complete medium, 20 μL 0.5 % MTT solution was injected to each well and continued to hatch for 4 h in incubator. To terminate cell incubation, 150 μL of DMSO solution was added into each well and shaken at low speed for 0.5 min after removing MTT solution. Ultimately, the optical density of 96-well plate was counted with microplate reader (BioTek Instruments Inc, ELX800).

The living MCF-7 cells were incubated in a complete culture medium and then incubated sequentially in a CO2 incubator for 12 h after dividing into 6-well plates. The PBS solution containing 200 μgmL−1 N/S-CDs was injected into the above 6-well plate and hatched for 9 h, and then the culture dish was washed 3 times with aseptic PBS solution. Finally, the cells imaging was obtained through laser confocal fluorescence microscopy.

The N/S-CDs were synthesized using 1,6-hexanediamine dihydrochloride as carbon sources in DMSO by simple microwave method (Fig. 1 ). The synthesized N/S-CDs displayed outstanding dispersibility and photoluminescence stability.

The morphology and size of the sample was measured by TEM. Fig. 2 a showed the particle size lay within the limits of 1–6.6 nm, and the average diameter of particles was about 4.35 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2b) implied that the N/S-CDs has a wider graphitic carbon diffraction peak (2θ = 23.8°) without other crystalline phases, illustrating that the sample has an amorphous carbon structure and other ingredients have been removed completely from the N/S-CDs [24]. The surface group composition of the N/S-CDs was further surveyed through XPS and FTIR. XPS complete spectrum confirmed that the CDs include carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulfur with four peaks at 286.3, 400.1, 532.8 and 197.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 2c). C1 s spectrum showed four peaks of CC/CC (284.6 eV), CS/C-N at (285.7 eV), CO (286.5 eV) and CO (288.5 eV) [25]. The N 1s spectrum has three main peaks, which were belonged to amino N (398.9 eV), pyrrolic N (400 eV) and graphite N (401.3 eV) [26]. Likewise, the O 1s peaks can be ascribed to CO (531.6 eV) and CO (532.8 eV) [27]. The S 2p spectrum contained four peaks: the peaks located at 164.4 and 165.5 eV were S 2p1/2, the peak at 162.9 eV amounted to S 2p3/2 and another peak was related to CSOxC at 167.7 eV [28]. The XPS results exhibited the N/S-CDs owned surface functional groups such as -NH2, CO, CN, CC and CS. The FTIR spectra (Fig.S2) indicated several representative peaks for the N/S-CDs. Peaks located at round 3410 and 3210 cm−1 could belonged to stretching mode of OH and NH2 [29]. A strong CH stretching vibration was observed at 2940 cm−1, and another peak located at 2086 cm−1 can correspond to the -SCN group [30]. Moreover, CO and CN vibrations could be found at 1649 cm−1 [31]. The CO and CS stretch was situated at 1123 cm−1, and bands at 1465 cm−1 could be related to CN group [32]. Accordingly, these results demonstrated the existing of CC, CO, NH2, OH and SCN chemical construction on the N/S-CDs.

The UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3 a) showed a classic fluorescenceabsorption peak at 270 nm which attributed to the CC (π−π*) of N/S-CDs. The peak was observed at 310 nm, corresponding to the (n−π*) transitions of CO and CN bonds [33]. Furthermore, interrelated PL emission curve of the synthesized CDs was measured and emerged a single emission peak (Fig. 3b). As informed in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence peak of the CDs exhibited excitation dependence, and fluorescence mission wavelength emerged the phenomenon of red-shifted as the excitation wavelength increased gradually. When excited at 460 nm wavelength, the as-prepared N/S-CDs revealed the most powerful fluorescence emission peak (λ em =512 nm) and showed intense green fluorescence. The results of PL spectra displayed that the sample showed blue, green, yellow and orange (λ em = 474, 512, 527 and 563 nm, respectively) in line with excited at 410, 460, 480 and 530 nm. In addition, the QY of N/S-CDs was calculated, and it has the QY of 24 % under 460 nm excitation by employing the Rhodamine 6 G as reference (Fig.S3 and TableS1).

The stability performance of the N/S-CDs was further measured. TGA (Fig.S4) showed that the CDs exhibited glorious thermal stability at < 250 °C with a weight loss of 8 %, corresponding to the evaporation of moisture [34]. In the range of 250 ∼ 500 °C, a rapid mass decrease of CDs was observed due to the pyrolysis of surface groups and core carbon [35]. The TGA results exhibited a good heat resistance of the N/S-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence emission intensity of N/S-CDs irradiated for 350 min at 365 nm fundamentally kept the same, which manifested the outstanding photostability of the N/S-CDs (Fig.S5). In addition, we further measured the fluorescence change of CDs at different pH values (pH = 1–13). Fig.S6 exhibited that the PL emission intensity of N/S-CDs obviously enhanced under the augment of acidity. When the pH increased from 6 to 13, the fluorescence strength kept almost stable at low-pH (6–10) while decreased gradually at high-pH (10–13). The N/S-CDs maintained fluorescence intensity in both acidic and slightly alkaline environments, suggesting that it was well compatible for bioimaging.

The N/S-CDs sensors have been applied to turn-off fluorescence detection of MnO4 − ions, where MnO4 − can bond to functional group of the N/S-CDs. Firstly, the selectivity of MnO4 − sensing against other anions (including Cl−, ClO4 −, ClO−, NO2 −, Cr2O7 2−, Br−, P2O7 4−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−) was also detected at 460 nm in same concentration (500 μM). Fig. 4 a confirmed the photoluminescence respond of CDs upon the accretion of these anions, and most anions only observed weak fluorescence change expect Cr2O7 2− and MnO4 − (the PL spectra was shown in Fig.S7).

Besides, the ion response towards Cr2O7 2− were performed. As shown in Fig.S8, the fluorescence intensity decreased with the addition of Cr2O7 2− and the fluorescence could be almost completely quenched in the presence of 500 μM Cr2O7 2−. The detection limit towards Cr2O7 2− was calculated to be about 0.23 μM, while its linearity is not satisfied (R2 = 0.9318). Therefore, this method is not suitable for the sensing of Cr2O7 2−.

In order to eliminate the influence of Cr2O7 2− on MnO4 −, we added excessive Pb2+ to the solution to precipitate Cr2O7 2−. Fig. 4d shows that the CDs mixed solution still has good fluorescence quenching effect on MnO4 − after addition of Pb2+, indicating that Cr2O7 2− has no effect on the PL response of MnO4 − with the presence of Pb2+ solution.

The detection ability of carbon dots toward MnO4 − at different pH was performed. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence of the CDs was distinctly quenched with 200 μM of KMnO4. Especially at pH 3.0 ∼ 7.0 buffer solution, the greater quenching efficiency could be obtained. In addition, the mechanism and kinetic behavior of MnO4 − sensing was studied. As shown in Figure S9, the absorption spectrum of the CDs showed significant change in the presence of KMnO4. The intensity of absorption bands of CDs at 230 ∼ 450 nm increased and the characteristic absorption bands of KMnO4 from 500 to 580 nm disappeared, suggesting the formation of complex by the reaction between CDs and KMnO4 that resulted in fluorescence quenching. Fig. S10 displays the kinetic behavior of MnO4 − sensing. When KMnO4 was added into the CDs solution, the fluorescence signal decrease sharply and reached 93 % of steady fluorescence value in 3 s. The results revealed that the quenching was a very rapid reaction.

Furthermore, the sensing capability of CDs to MnO4 − was studied. Fig. 4b was the PL intensity change spectra of N/S-CDs with various concentrations of MnO4 − (0−600 μM), showing that the fluorescence intensity decline constantly with the rise of the concentration of MnO4 −. In addition, Fig. 4c showed the linear relationship between PL quenching and MnO4 − concentrations, and it displayed a good liner relationship with the MnO4 − in the range of 1–20 μM (Fig. 4c). As a result, the concentrations of MnO4 − could be fitted using the following equation:
(
F
o
− F ) /
F o
= 0.0081
C
( M n
O
4
−
)
( μ M )
+ 0.1027
(
R
2
= 0.994 )
Where the Fo and F are PL intensity of CDs before and after adding MnO4 −, respectively. According to the equation, therefore, the LOD of MnO4 − is 0.34 μM based on three times the standard deviation of the obtained signal at the lowest sample concentration divided by slope of the calibration curve.

After 24 h of co-incubated with the N/S-CDs and MCF-7 cells (Fig.S11), over 81 % of the MCF-7 cells hold biological activity even at 400 μg mL−1 of N/S-CDs, and it manifested that N/S-CDs have quite low cytotoxicity. After incubation with 200 μg mL−1 of the N/S-CDs for 9 h, the whole cells could transmitted intense blue, green, yellow and orange fluorescence under at 410, 460, 480 and 530 nm wavelength of laser excitation (Fig.5 ), respectively. It illustrated that the CDs can get into the cells adequately, implying that N/S-CDs have excellent bio-imaging ability.

In conclusion, one-step microwave method was utilized for the simple synthesis of N/S-CDs with respectable QY of 24 %, and these synthesized carbon dots have excellent optical properties and emit blue, green, yellow and orange fluorescence as the excitation wavelength increases. The N/S-CDs has excellent thermal stability and can further enhance fluorescence emission under medium and acidic conditions. Then, the N/S-CDs exhibited certain selectivity for MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−, and the limits of detection are 0.23 μM and 0.34 μM, respectively. Moreover, the obtained N/S-CDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility and could be applied for cellular imaging.

Jiucun Chen, Yanzi Jin and Zhiqin Deng conceived and designed the study and wrote the manuscript. Caihe Ding and Zhiqin Deng performed all the synthesis and characterization experiments. Yanzi Jin revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities (SWU118035 and XDJK2019B001), the Transformative Project for Excellent Scientific and Technological Achievements in University (KJZH17108) and the Special Program for Chongqing Social Business and People’s Livelihood Guarantee of Science and Technology (cstc2017shmsA30001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2020.110838.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
